description: Review rubric for deciding which color-language layers are stable future-proof contracts versus bounded compatibility, calibration, migration, or generated deliverables.

questions.prompt: Is this layer free of hidden design authority drift?
questions.intent: The layer should either remain platform-free or act only as a translation boundary, without re-owning palette authorship.

questions.prompt: Can multiple schemes reuse this layer without rewriting generators?
questions.intent: Future-proof contracts should support new schemes as data changes, not as generator forks.

questions.prompt: Can downstream results still explain this layer in lineage and parity?
questions.intent: A future-proof layer must remain visible in reports and not disappear into opaque postprocess behavior.

questions.prompt: Do source-layer and registry audits guard this boundary?
questions.intent: Stable contracts should be enforceable, not just documented.

questions.prompt: Is this the right place for maintainers to edit intent directly?
questions.intent: Future-proof contracts should stay editable at the source instead of forcing maintainers into generated or migration files.

questions.prompt: Is the exception truly host- or renderer-specific?
questions.intent: Only residual behavior that is not worth abstracting yet should live here.

questions.prompt: Is the exception surface area intentionally small and measurable?
questions.intent: Bounded compatibility should stay narrow so the abstract model keeps growing instead of leaking.

questions.prompt: Does the entry explain why abstraction is not worth it yet?
questions.intent: Every compatibility escape hatch needs an explicit cost-benefit explanation.

questions.prompt: Is the boundary free of reusable design values?
questions.intent: This layer may explain exceptions, but it must not quietly become a palette-definition layer.

questions.prompt: Is there a clear trigger for revisiting this exception later?
questions.intent: Bounded compatibility should be reviewable, not forgotten.

questions.prompt: Is this layer limited to bounded compensation rather than design authorship?
questions.intent: Calibration exists to tune readability and parity, not to redefine the scheme.

questions.prompt: Would removing this file still leave the design authority intact upstream?
questions.intent: A calibration layer should never become the only place where intent lives.

questions.prompt: Can the effects of calibration still be explained in lineage or parity?
questions.intent: Calibration should remain observable and bounded.

questions.prompt: Are the adjustments grounded in explicit budgets or thresholds?
questions.intent: Calibration should follow measurable constraints instead of taste-based drift.

questions.prompt: Is the file sync-managed rather than hand-authored design input?
questions.intent: Migration anchors should preserve compatibility while upstream layers mature.

questions.prompt: Is it clear which abstract layer should absorb this anchor over time?
questions.intent: Migration anchors need an exit path, not indefinite limbo.

questions.prompt: Is the remaining anchor surface visible in residual or compatibility reporting?
questions.intent: Migration work should stay measurable.

questions.prompt: Is there a defined condition for declaring the migration complete?
questions.intent: This keeps migration layers from quietly becoming permanent authorities.

questions.prompt: Can the output be fully reproduced by sync or build?
questions.intent: Generated deliverables should be deterministic artifacts.

questions.prompt: Do source and generator changes fully explain output drift?
questions.intent: Generated files should never become hidden sources of truth.

questions.prompt: Is the team protected from editing this output directly?
questions.intent: Generated artifacts must stay clearly downstream.

questions.prompt: Do audits and sync checks cover this output class?
questions.intent: Generated artifacts should be guarded by automation, not memory.

assessments.summary: Scheme identity, philosophy, and taxonomy now behave as explicit public-facing contracts instead of sharing authority with platform files.
assessments.nextAction: Keep this layer public-facing and platform-free.

assessments.summary: Product manifests now own distribution identity, preview copy, and release-facing metadata without re-owning palette authorship.
assessments.nextAction: Keep marketplace and preview identity here, while leaving color intent in scheme/core layers.

assessments.summary: Families, semantics, surfaces, guidance, interface, interaction, feedback, and terminal language now live in abstract scheme data instead of host-specific files.
assessments.nextAction: Keep preferring sparse anchors and derivation over per-variant result tables.

assessments.summary: Climate behavior and intensity knobs are now separated from semantic identity, so new schemes can reuse the framework without rewriting generators.
assessments.nextAction: Use this layer whenever the grammar stays the same but climate intensity changes.

assessments.summary: Adapters and VS Code chrome bindings now behave as translation contracts rather than hidden design sources.
assessments.nextAction: Add new hosts here only after the abstract layer already expresses the needed behavior.

assessments.summary: The remaining residual workbench keys are explicit host-specific exceptions with rationale, instead of silent leftovers.
assessments.nextAction: Revisit when a new abstract tone role can replace one of the residual keys cleanly.

assessments.summary: Calibration is fenced into bounded tuning logic and no longer acts as a hidden design-definition layer.
assessments.nextAction: Keep tuning numeric and budget-driven; move any new color identity work upward instead.

assessments.summary: Source and template snapshots are still required for compatibility, but they are sync-managed migration anchors with a visible exit path.
assessments.nextAction: Shrink this surface only when abstraction cost stays lower than host-specific complexity.

assessments.summary: Themes, docs, reports, and derived assets are reproducible deliverables guarded by sync and generated-origin checks.
assessments.nextAction: Treat drift as a source-layer or generator problem, never as a hand-editing task.
